## Question
Assume the consistency strength of a measurable cardinal. A \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilter is an ultrafilter closed under countable intersections. Using the standard comparison notions \(<_L\) (the Lipschitz order) and \(<_k\) (the Ketonen order) on \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters, where “Ketonen-incomparable” means that neither ultrafilter is below the other in \(<_k\), and writing WUA for the Weak Ultrapower Axiom, what is the strongest statement that can be proved about consistency phenomena for \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters?

## Choices
A. Relative to a measurable cardinal, it is consistent that the Lipschitz and Ketonen orders on \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters can fail to coincide, but only outside the Weak Ultrapower Axiom; under WUA, the Ketonen order is always linear on \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters.
B. Relative to a measurable cardinal, it is consistent that there exist two \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) such that \(\mathcal V\) is Lipschitz below \(\mathcal W\), while \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) are Ketonen-incomparable; however, no model satisfying the Weak Ultrapower Axiom can contain such a pair.
C. Relative to a measurable cardinal, it is consistent that there exist two \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) such that \(\mathcal V\) is Lipschitz below \(\mathcal W\) and the Lipschitz and Ketonen orders do not coincide on \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters; nevertheless, in every model of the Weak Ultrapower Axiom, any relation \(\mathcal V<_L\mathcal W\) already implies that \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) are comparable in the Ketonen order.
D. Relative to a measurable cardinal, it is consistent that there exist two \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) such that \(\mathcal V\) is Lipschitz below \(\mathcal W\), while \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) are Ketonen-incomparable.
E. Relative to a measurable cardinal, it is consistent that there exist two \(\sigma\)-complete ultrafilters \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) such that \(\mathcal V\) is Lipschitz below \(\mathcal W\), while \(\mathcal V\) and \(\mathcal W\) are Ketonen-incomparable; moreover, it is also consistent that such a situation occurs together with the Weak Ultrapower Axiom.

## Question
Let \((M,g)\) be a complete noncompact \(n\)-dimensional Riemannian manifold such that \(\operatorname{Ric}\ge \lambda g\) for some \(\lambda\in\mathbb{R}\) and \(\operatorname{inj}(M,g)\ge i>0\). For \(u\in W^{1,n}(M)\), define \(\|u\|_{1,\tau}:=\|\nabla_g u\|_{L^n(M)}+\tau\|u\|_{L^n(M)}\), let \(\phi_n(t)=e^t-\sum_{k=0}^{n-2} t^k/k!\), let \(\alpha_n=n\omega_{n-1}^{1/(n-1)}\) where \(\omega_{n-1}\) is the surface measure of the unit sphere \(S^{n-1}\subset\mathbb{R}^n\), let \(dV_g\) denote the Riemannian volume element, and let \(r_H\) denote the harmonic radius of \((M,g)\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the associated Moser--Trudinger functional on \(M\)?

## Choices
A. There exists a constant \(\epsilon=\epsilon(\lambda,i,n)>0\), with \(\epsilon=O(1/r_H)\), such that for every \(\tau>\epsilon\) there is a constant \(C=C(n,\tau,M)\) for which
\[
\sup_{u\in W^{1,n}(M),\ \|u\|_{1,\tau}\le 1}\int_M \exp\!\left(\alpha_n |u|^{\frac{n}{n-1}}\right)\,dV_g\le C.
\]
Moreover, the constant \(\alpha_n\) is sharp.
B. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
C. There exists a constant \(\epsilon=\epsilon(\lambda,i,n)>0\), with \(\epsilon=O(1/r_H)\), such that for every \(\tau>\epsilon\) there is a constant \(C=C(n,\tau,\lambda,i)\), independent of the particular manifold \(M\), for which
\[
\sup_{u\in W^{1,n}(M),\ \|u\|_{1,\tau}\le 1}\int_M \phi_n\!\left(\alpha_n |u|^{\frac{n}{n-1}}\right)\,dV_g\le C.
\]
Moreover, the constant \(\alpha_n\) is sharp.
D. There exists a constant \(\epsilon=\epsilon(\lambda,i,n)>0\), with \(\epsilon=O(1/r_H)\), such that for every \(\tau>0\) there is a constant \(C=C(n,\tau,M)\) for which
\[
\sup_{u\in W^{1,n}(M),\ \|u\|_{1,\tau}\le 1}\int_M \phi_n\!\left(\alpha_n |u|^{\frac{n}{n-1}}\right)\,dV_g\le C.
\]
Moreover, the constant \(\alpha_n\) is sharp.
E. There exists a constant \(\epsilon=\epsilon(\lambda,i,n)>0\), with \(\epsilon=O(1/r_H)\), such that for every \(\tau>\epsilon\) and every \(0\le \alpha<\alpha_n\) there is a constant \(C=C(n,\tau,M)\) for which
\[
\sup_{u\in W^{1,n}(M),\ \|u\|_{1,\tau}\le 1}\int_M \phi_n\!\left(\alpha |u|^{\frac{n}{n-1}}\right)\,dV_g\le C.
\]

## Question
Let \(q(G)\) denote the largest eigenvalue of the signless Laplacian of a graph \(G\). Suppose \(G\) is a connected outerplanar graph of order \(n\) (that is, with \(n\) vertices) that is \(C_\ell\)-free, where \(\ell\ge 3\). Let \(P_t\) be the path on \(t\) vertices, let \(\alpha P_{\ell-2}\) mean the disjoint union of \(\alpha\) copies of \(P_{\ell-2}\), let \(\cup\) denote disjoint union, and let \(\vee\) denote graph join. Define
\[
\alpha=\left\lfloor \frac{n-1}{\ell-2}\right\rfloor,\qquad r=n-1-\alpha(\ell-2),
\]
and, if \(r=0\), interpret \(P_r=P_0\) as the empty graph. Which quantitative estimate holds for \(q(G)\)?

## Choices
A. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
B. \(q(G)\le q\bigl(K_1\vee (\alpha P_{\ell-2}\cup P_r)\bigr)\).
C. \(q(G)\le q\bigl(K_1\vee (\alpha P_{\ell-2}\cup rP_1)\bigr)\), where \(rP_1\) denotes the disjoint union of \(r\) copies of \(P_1\); with equality if and only if \(G\cong K_1\vee (\alpha P_{\ell-2}\cup rP_1)\).
D. \(q(G)\le q\bigl(K_1\vee (\alpha P_{\ell-2}\cup P_r)\bigr)\), and equality holds whenever \(G\) has the same degree sequence as \(K_1\vee (\alpha P_{\ell-2}\cup P_r)\).
E. \(q(G)\le q\bigl(K_1\vee (\alpha P_{\ell-2}\cup P_r)\bigr)\), with equality if and only if \(G\) has a dominating vertex \(v\) such that the components of \(G-v\) have orders \(\ell-2,\ldots,\ell-2,r\).

## Question
A consistently oriented double ray is a two-way infinite directed path whose edges all point coherently along the path, and a one-ended digraph is a digraph whose underlying undirected graph has exactly one end. Here, saying that the consistently oriented double ray is ubiquitous means that whenever a digraph contains, for every n\in\mathbb{N}, n pairwise disjoint copies of a consistently oriented double ray, it must contain infinitely many pairwise disjoint such copies. Which statement is equivalent to saying that the consistently oriented double ray is ubiquitous?

## Choices
A. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
B. The consistently oriented double ray is ubiquitous when restricted to the class of strongly connected one-ended digraphs.
C. The consistently oriented double ray is ubiquitous when restricted to the class of one-ended digraphs in which every vertex has finite indegree and finite outdegree.
D. If the consistently oriented double ray is ubiquitous, then it is ubiquitous when restricted to the class of one-ended digraphs.
E. For every fixed n\in\mathbb{N}, every one-ended digraph containing n pairwise disjoint copies of a consistently oriented double ray contains infinitely many pairwise disjoint such copies.

## Question
Let $(M,\varphi)$ be a diffuse separable $\mathrm{W}^*$-probability space (that is, a von Neumann algebra with a faithful normal state). Recall that $(M,\varphi)$ is called selfless if the first-factor inclusion $(M,\varphi)\subset (M,\varphi)\ast (M,\varphi)$, where $\ast$ denotes the free product of $\mathrm{W}^*$-probability spaces, is existentially closed; and that an inclusion $(M,\varphi)\subset (N,\psi)$ is existentially closed if for some nonprincipal ultrafilter $\mathcal U$ one has $(M,\varphi)\subset (N,\psi)\subset (M,\varphi)^{\mathcal U}$ via the diagonal inclusion. Which statement gives a complete characterization of when $(M,\varphi)$ is selfless?

## Choices
A. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
B. Exactly that the free product $(M,\varphi) \ast (\operatorname{L}(\mathbb{Z}),\tau_{\mathbb{Z}})$ itself is selfless.
C. Exactly that for every nontrivial $\mathrm{W}^*$-probability space $(N,\psi)$, the first-factor inclusion $(M,\varphi) \subset (M,\varphi) \ast (N,\psi)$ is existentially closed.
D. It is characterized by the existence of some nontrivial $\mathrm{W}^*$-probability space $(N,\psi)$ such that the first-factor inclusion $(M,\varphi) \subset (M,\varphi) \ast (N,\psi)$ is existentially closed.
E. Exactly that the first-factor inclusion $(M,\varphi) \subset (M,\varphi) \ast (\operatorname{L}(\mathbb{Z}),\tau_{\mathbb{Z}})$ is existentially closed for every nonprincipal ultrafilter $\mathcal U$, in the sense that $(M,\varphi) \ast (\operatorname{L}(\mathbb{Z}),\tau_{\mathbb{Z}})$ embeds into $(M,\varphi)^{\mathcal U}$ over $(M,\varphi)$ via the diagonal inclusion.

## Question
Let $p\ge 1$, let $\mathcal S(\mathbb R^2)$ denote the Schwartz class on $\mathbb R^2$, and let $\|\cdot\|_p$ denote the usual $L^p(\mathbb R^2)$ norm. For $t>0$, define the circular averaging operators
\[
A_t f(x)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} f(x_1-t\cos\alpha,\,x_2-t\sin\alpha)\,d\alpha,
\]
and let $V_2^I A f$ denote the $2$-variation in the parameter $t$ over a fixed interval $I$ of the family $\{A_t f\}_{t\in I}$. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about $V_2^I A f$ acting on $f\in \mathcal S(\mathbb R^2)$?

## Choices
A. For every $p>2$, 
\[
\sup_{\substack{f\in \mathcal S(\mathbb R^2)\\ \|f\|_p\le 1}} \|V_2^I A f\|_{L^p(\mathbb R^2)}=\infty,
\]
whereas for every $1\le p\le 2$ one has
\[
\sup_{\substack{f\in \mathcal S(\mathbb R^2)\\ \|f\|_p\le 1}} \|V_2^I A f\|_{L^p(\mathbb R^2)}<\infty.
\]
B. Whenever $I$ is a fixed compact subinterval of $(0,\infty)$, the smooth dependence of $A_t f$ on $t$ implies that for every $p\ge 1$ there is a constant $C_{p,I}<\infty$ such that
\[
\|V_2^I A f\|_{L^p(\mathbb R^2)}\le C_{p,I}\,\|f\|_{L^p(\mathbb R^2)}
\quad\text{for all }f\in \mathcal S(\mathbb R^2).
\]
C. For every $p>2$, 
\[
\sup_{\substack{f\in \mathcal S(\mathbb R^2)\\ \|f\|_p\le 1}} \|V_2^I A f\|_{L^p(\mathbb R^2)}=\infty.
\]
D. For every $p\ge 1$, \[\sup_{\substack{f\in \mathcal S(\mathbb R^2)\\ \|f\|_p\le 1}} \|V_2^I A f\|_{L^p(\mathbb R^2)}=\infty.\]
E. For every $p>2$ there exist constants $C_p<\infty$ and $\lambda_0(p)\ge 1$ such that, whenever $\lambda\ge \lambda_0(p)$ and $f\in \mathcal S(\mathbb R^2)$ satisfies $\|f\|_p\le 1$ and $\operatorname{supp}\widehat f\subseteq \{\xi\in\mathbb R^2:|\xi|\le \lambda\}$, one has
\[
\|V_2^I A f\|_{L^p(\mathbb R^2)}\le C_p\,(\log \lambda)^{\frac14-\frac1{2p}}.
\]

## Question
Let U \subset \mathbb{R}^d be an open set, possibly unbounded, with d \ge 2. Let \mathbf{G} \in L^2_{loc}(U,\mathbb{R}^d) and c \in L^1_{loc}(U). Let A=(a_{ij})_{1\le i,j\le d} be a symmetric matrix field such that each coefficient a_{ij} belongs to VMO_{loc}(U), meaning it has vanishing mean oscillation on every ball whose closure is contained in U. Assume also that the distributional divergence of A belongs to L^2_{loc}(U,\mathbb{R}^d), where \operatorname{div}A=\mathbf E means
\[
\int_U \sum_{i,j=1}^d a_{ij}\,\partial_i \psi_j\,dx=-\int_U \sum_{j=1}^d E_j\,\psi_j\,dx
\qquad \text{for all } (\psi_1,\dots,\psi_d)\in C_c^\infty(U)^d.
\]
Assume further that A is locally uniformly strictly elliptic and locally bounded on U: for every open ball V with \overline V\subset U, there exist constants \lambda_V,M_V>0 such that
\[
\lambda_V|\xi|^2\le \langle A(x)\xi,\xi\rangle,\qquad \max_{i,j}|a_{ij}(x)|\le M_V
\]
for a.e. x\in V and all \xi\in\mathbb{R}^d. Let \tilde{\mathbf F}\in L^2_{loc}(U,\mathbb{R}^d) and \tilde f\in L^1_{loc}(U). Suppose h\in L^\infty_{loc}(U) satisfies
\[
\int_U \big(\operatorname{trace}(A\nabla^2\varphi)+\langle \mathbf G,\nabla\varphi\rangle + c\varphi\big)h\,dx
= \int_U \tilde f\,\varphi\,dx + \int_U \langle \tilde{\mathbf F},\nabla\varphi\rangle\,dx
\qquad \text{for all }\varphi\in C_c^\infty(U),
\]
where \operatorname{trace}(A\nabla^2\varphi)=\sum_{i,j=1}^d a_{ij}\,\partial_{ij}\varphi. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about every such h?

## Choices
A. h belongs to L^2_{loc}(U).
B. h belongs to H^{2,2}_{loc}(U).
C. h belongs to H^{1,2}_{loc}(U), and for every open ball V with \overline V\subset U there exists a constant C_V depending only on the local coefficients and data such that \|h\|_{H^{1,2}(V)}\le C_V\big(\|\tilde{\mathbf F}\|_{L^2(V)}+\|\tilde f\|_{L^1(V)}\big).
D. h has a representative that is locally Hölder continuous on U.
E. h belongs to H^{1,2}_{loc}(U).

## Question
Let \(p\) be an odd prime, let \(\mathbb F_p\) be the field of \(p\) elements, and let \(c\) be an extra vertex not in \(\mathbb F_p\). For \(x\in \mathbb F_p\), call \(x\) a quadratic residue if \(x=a^2\) for some \(a\in \mathbb F_p\), and a quadratic non-residue otherwise among \(\mathbb F_p^*=\mathbb F_p\setminus\{0\}\); regard \(0\) as a quadratic residue and \(c\) as a quadratic non-residue. A 1-factor of \(K_{p+1}\) is a perfect matching on the vertex set \(\mathbb F_p \cup \{c\}\). For an edge \(\{x,y\}\) with \(x,y\in \mathbb F_p\), define its length to be \(\min\{(x-y) \bmod p,(y-x) \bmod p\}\), where residues modulo \(p\) are taken in \(\{0,1,\dots,p-1\}\); for an edge \(\{x,c\}\), define its length to be \(0\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about 1-factors of \(K_{p+1}\) for every odd prime \(p\) under the condition that every edge joins a quadratic residue to a quadratic non-residue?

## Choices
A. There exists a 1-factor \(F\) of \(K_{p+1}\) with vertex set \(\mathbb F_p \cup \{c\}\) such that every edge of \(F\) joins a quadratic residue to a quadratic non-residue.
B. There exists a 1-factor \(F\) of \(K_{p+1}\) with vertex set \(\mathbb F_p \cup \{c\}\) such that every edge of \(F\) joins a quadratic residue to a quadratic non-residue, the lengths of the edges in \(F\) are pairwise distinct, and for each \(t\in\mathbb F_p\), the matching obtained from \(F\) by replacing each finite vertex \(x\) with \(x+t\) modulo \(p\) and fixing \(c\) also has every edge joining a quadratic residue to a quadratic non-residue.
C. For every odd prime \(p\), whenever \(F\) is a 1-factor of \(K_{p+1}\) such that every edge of \(F\) joins a quadratic residue to a quadratic non-residue and the lengths of the edges in \(F\) are pairwise distinct, the unique edge of \(F\) incident to \(c\) must be \(\{c,0\}\).
D. There exists a 1-factor \(F\) of \(K_{p+1}\) with vertex set \(\mathbb F_p \cup \{c\}\) such that every edge of \(F\) joins a quadratic residue to a quadratic non-residue, and the lengths of the edges in \(F\) are pairwise distinct.
E. For every odd prime \(p\), every 1-factor \(F\) of \(K_{p+1}\) with vertex set \(\mathbb F_p \cup \{c\}\) such that every edge of \(F\) joins a quadratic residue to a quadratic non-residue already has pairwise distinct lengths.

## Question
Let \(T>1\) be a fixed transseries, and let \(\mathbb{R}\wspc{T}\) denote the corresponding field of omega-series in \(T\). Let \(\mathbb{U}^{>\mathbb{R}}\) be the ordered set of inputs \(x\) for which the composition \(f\circ x\) is defined. For a given \(f\in \mathbb{R}\wspc{T}\), consider the map \(x\mapsto f\circ x\) on \(\mathbb{U}^{>\mathbb{R}}\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the monotonicity of this map when \(f'\) is everywhere positive, everywhere negative, or identically zero?

## Choices
A. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
B. The map \(x\mapsto f\circ x\) is strictly increasing if \(f'>0\), strictly decreasing if \(f'<0\), and only locally constant on \(\mathbb{U}^{>\mathbb{R}}\) if \(f'=0\), with no global constancy conclusion in general.
C. If \(f'>0\), then the map \(x\mapsto f\circ x\) is increasing; if \(f'<0\), then it is decreasing; and if \(f'=0\), then it is constant.
D. The sign of \(f'\) determines the monotonicity of \(x\mapsto f\circ x\) only for those \(f\) that satisfy an additional weak mean-value estimate; for arbitrary omega-series, the derivative sign alone does not suffice.
E. The sign of \(f'\) gives the stated monotonicity conclusion for omega-series obtained from \(T\) by finitely many applications of \(\exp\) and \(\log\), but for genuinely infinite omega-series one can in general conclude only non-strict monotonicity.

## Question
Let $\Omega$ be a nondegenerate skew-symmetric form on $\mathbb{C}^4$, and let
$$F=\{(V_1,V_2)\in \mathrm{G}(1,4)\times \mathrm{G}(2,4)\mid V_1\subset V_2,\ \text{and }V_1,V_2\text{ are }\Omega\text{-isotropic}\}$$
be the complete flag variety of $\Omega$-isotropic lines contained in $\Omega$-isotropic planes in $\mathbb{C}^4$. Let $H\subset \mathrm{PSP}(4)$, where $\mathrm{PSP}(4)$ is the projective symplectic group of $\Omega$, be the complex torus of homothety classes of diagonal matrices acting naturally on $F$. What is the normalized Chow quotient $X$ of $F$ by $H$?

## Choices
A. A singular del Pezzo surface of degree 4 with two singularities of type $A_2$.
B. A singular del Pezzo surface of degree 5 with two singularities of type $A_1$.
C. A singular del Pezzo surface of degree 4 with one singularity of type $A_1$ and one singularity of type $A_2$.
D. A singular del Pezzo surface of degree 4 with two singularities of type $A_1$.
E. A singular del Pezzo surface of degree 4.

## Question
Let $(M,g)$ be a Lorentzian manifold of dimension at least $3$. Assume that $M$ is locally conformally homogeneous, meaning that its local conformal pseudogroup (the collection of conformal diffeomorphisms between open subsets of $M$) acts transitively on $M$. Which description gives the complete case-by-case classification of such manifolds?

## Choices
A. Every such $(M,g)$ satisfies at least one of these three alternatives: the local conformal pseudogroup preserves some Einstein Lorentzian metric in the conformal class $[g]$ of $g$; or $(M,g)$ is conformally flat; or $(M,g)$ is locally conformally equivalent to an isometrically locally homogeneous plane wave, equivalently to a homogeneous plane wave $X$ in the sense that every point of $M$ has a neighborhood conformally diffeomorphic to an open subset of $X$.
B. Every such $(M,g)$ satisfies at least one of these three alternatives: the local conformal pseudogroup preserves some Lorentzian metric in the conformal class $[g]$ of $g$; or $(M,g)$ is conformally flat; or the universal cover of $M$ is locally conformally equivalent to an isometrically locally homogeneous plane wave, equivalently to a homogeneous plane wave $X$ in the sense that every point of the universal cover has a neighborhood conformally diffeomorphic to an open subset of $X$.
C. Every such $(M,g)$ satisfies at least one of these three alternatives: the local conformal pseudogroup preserves some Lorentzian metric in the conformal class $[g]$ of $g$; or $(M,g)$ is conformally flat; or $(M,g)$ is locally conformally equivalent to an isometrically locally homogeneous plane wave, equivalently to a homogeneous plane wave $X$ in the sense that every point of $M$ has a neighborhood conformally diffeomorphic to an open subset of $X$.
D. Every such $(M,g)$ satisfies at least one of these three alternatives: the local conformal pseudogroup preserves some Lorentzian metric in the conformal class $[g]$ of $g$; or $(M,g)$ is conformally flat; or $(M,g)$ is locally conformally equivalent to a plane wave, in the sense that every point of $M$ has a neighborhood conformally diffeomorphic to an open subset of some plane wave.
E. Every such $(M,g)$ satisfies at least one of these three alternatives: the local conformal pseudogroup preserves some Lorentzian metric in the conformal class $[g]$ of $g$; or $(M,g)$ is conformally flat; or $(M,g)$ is locally conformally equivalent to a locally symmetric plane wave, equivalently to a homogeneous plane wave $X$ with parallel curvature tensor such that every point of $M$ has a neighborhood conformally diffeomorphic to an open subset of $X$.

## Question
Let \(\Omega\subset \mathbb R^n\) be open, let \(0<T<\infty\), and write \(\Omega_T=\Omega\times(0,T)\). Let \(E\subset \Omega_T\) be closed, and let \(\mathcal A(x,t,\xi)\) satisfy for some constants \(\nu,L>0\) and \(p>1\)
\[
\mathcal A(x,t,\xi)\cdot \xi\ge \nu |\xi|^p,
\qquad |
\mathcal A(x,t,\xi)|\le L|\xi|^{p-1},
\]
and the strict monotonicity condition
\[
\bigl(\mathcal A(x,t,\xi)-\mathcal A(x,t,\eta)\bigr)\cdot(\xi-\eta)>0
\quad\text{for all }\xi\ne\eta\text{ in }\mathbb R^n.
\]
Assume \(u\in C(\Omega_T)\) is a weak solution of
\[
\partial_t u-\operatorname{div}\mathcal A(x,t,\nabla u)=0
\]
in \(\Omega_T\setminus E\), and that for some \(M>0\) and \(\alpha>0\),
\[
|u(z)-u(z_0)|\le M\, d_{\alpha}(z,z_0)^{\alpha}
\]
for every \(z_0\in E\) and \(z\in \Omega_T\), where for \(z=(x,t)\) and \(z_0=(y,s)\)
\[
d_{\alpha}(z,z_0)=\max\Big\{|x-y|,\ |t-s|^{1/(p+\alpha(2-p))}\Big\},
\]
and \(\mathcal H^{\sigma}_{(\alpha)}\) is the Hausdorff measure associated with coverings by cylinders
\[

Let \(g\in C^\infty(\mathbb R)\) with \(g(0)=0\). For \(s>0\), define

where \(\partial_x^{-1}f\) is the Fourier multiplier with symbol \((i\xi)^{-1}\). Let \(s\ge 2\), let \(u_0\in X^s(\mathbb R^2)\), and let
\[
 u\in C([0,T_{u_0}^*);X^s(\mathbb R^2))\cap C^1([0,T_{u_0}^*);X^{s-2}(\mathbb R^2))
\]
be the unique solution on its maximal existence interval \([0,T_{u_0}^*)\) of
\[
\begin{cases}
u_t+\gamma u u_x+G*\partial_x\left(\frac12 g(u)+\frac\gamma2 u_x^2-\frac\gamma2 u^2\right)+G*v_y=0,\quad (x,y)\in\mathbb R^2,\\
\nu_y=v_x,\\
u\big|_{t=0}=u_0(x,y),\quad (x,y)\in\mathbb R^2.
\end{cases}
\]
Assume that the maximal existence time is finite, \(T_{u_0}^*<\infty\). Writing \(\nabla u=(u_x,u_y)\), what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the blow-up behavior of \(u\) as \(t\to T_{u_0}^{*-}\)?

\]
E. Necessarily,
\[

\]

## Question
Let \(\mu\) be a signed Radon measure on \(\mathbb{R}^n\). Say that \(\mu\) has a unique blow-up at \(x\) if there exists a Radon measure \(\mu_x\) such that, for every sequence \(r_j\to 0^+\), after passing to a subsequence one has
\[
\mu_{x,r_j}:=\frac{1}{|\mu|(B_{r_j}(x))}(\tau_{x,r_j})_\#\mu \overset{*}{\rightharpoonup} c\,\mu_x
\]
for some constant \(c\neq 0\), where \(\tau_{x,r}(y)=(y-x)/r\) and \(|\mu|\) is the total variation measure of \(\mu\). For points \(x\) with a unique blow-up, let \(D_x\subset \mathbb{R}^n\) denote the distinguished linear subspace associated with that blow-up, and for \(k\in\{0,1,\dots,n\}\) define the \(k\)-stratum by
\[
\mathcal S_\mu^k:=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^n:\text{\(\mu\) has a unique blow-up at \(x\) and }\dim D_x=k\}.
\]
A \(k\)-dimensional Lipschitz graph means the graph of a Lipschitz map from a \(k\)-dimensional affine subspace into its orthogonal complement. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the structure of \(\mathcal S_\mu^k\) and its tangent planes under the assumptions above?

## Choices

B. \(\mathcal S_\mu^k\) is contained in a countable union of \(k\)-dimensional Lipschitz graphs, and for \(\mathcal H^k\)-almost every \(x\in \mathcal S_\mu^k\), the approximate tangent \(k\)-plane to \(\mathcal S_\mu^k\) at \(x\) exists and is exactly \(D_x\).
